quotes.verbatim_quote:
The
LYDIAN AUGMENTED SCALE is an extremely useful scale in this respect. It has two kinds of
application to dominant seventh chords, one resulting in the addition of a flatted fifth (or aug-
mented eleventh) and the other application supplying an augmented fifth, a flatted ninth, an aug-
mented ninth, and an augmented eleventh.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This defines the scale's two distinct harmonic functions over dominant seventh chords, which determines when and how a player chooses to deploy it.

quotes.verbatim_quote: In the case of the dominant 7th, flat 5th (b 5), the root of the scale is the seventh of the chord.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This rule specifies the exact scale-to-chord alignment for the first application, telling the player where to start the scale relative to the chord root.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
In the case of the dominant 7th, raised 5th, raised 9th, lowered 9th, and augmented 11th, the root
of the scale is the third of the chord.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This rule specifies the second, distinct scale-to-chord alignment for the altered-extensions application, requiring a different starting pitch than the first use case.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
There are 12 lydian augmented scales.

Learn all twelve scales by playing them over and over throughout the range of your instrument.
Then learn them as you did the major scale (by playing them in thirds, etc.). Be sure to practice
the scales in all twelve keys.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This is the method's explicit practice prescription, establishing both the scope (all twelve) and the method (range + intervallic patterns) for internalizing the scale.
